issues_found: The baseline logistic regression is described as using 'only the latest-visit structured features' in the hypothesis, but Section 4 clarifies it actually receives 24-month history features (prior admission/ED counts, time since last admission, medication count in prior 90 days). This is internally inconsistent and makes the experiment test a different hypothesis than stated. The hypothesis claims to isolate longitudinal sequence encoding vs. static snapshot features, but the baseline is partially longitudinal. This needs to be resolved: either the hypothesis should be reworded to reflect a history-aware LR baseline, or a true latest-visit-only LR should be used (with the history-aware LR as an ablation).

The structured features given to the logistic regression baseline include 'medication count in prior 90 days' and 'high-risk medication class indicators' drawn from the same pharmacy fill history the transformer uses as a sequence. This means the baseline has some access to medication history information, partially collapsing the intended architectural contrast. The experiment should be explicit about whether the baseline receives any derived medication features, and if so, add a dedicated ablation that removes them from the baseline to cleanly isolate the sequence-encoding contribution.

Absolute positional encodings are specified for the transformer EHR sequence. For irregular clinical time series where events are unevenly spaced over 24 months, absolute positional encodings (which typically encode sequence index position, not real calendar time) are a poor fit and can misrepresent temporal structure. Relative time encodings or continuous-time embeddings (e.g., time-since-last-event or sinusoidal encodings over real elapsed time) would be more appropriate. The design should justify this choice or switch to time-aware encodings.

The design mentions a '1-month burn-in buffer' between train/validation and validation/test splits to reduce contamination from overlapping patient cohorts, but does not specify whether patients who have discharges in both the training and validation periods are treated as separate index events or whether their training-period discharges are excluded to prevent patient-level leakage. If the same patient can appear in both training and test sets (at different discharge dates), the model may partially memorize patient-specific patterns, inflating apparent generalization. The design should state explicitly whether patient-level deduplication or holdout is enforced across splits.

AUROC is selected as the primary metric partly on the grounds that it is 'less sensitive to month-to-month variation in readmission prevalence.' However, with a temporal holdout the prevalence in the test set (months 31–36) may differ materially from training prevalence due to seasonal trends, policy changes, or pandemic-era effects in a 36-month window. AUROC does not reflect calibration, and a model that is well-ranked but poorly calibrated could be clinically misleading. While calibration metrics are listed as secondary, the design should acknowledge this limitation explicitly and consider whether AUPRC or net benefit should co-primary given the class imbalance typical of 30-day readmission (~15% base rate).

The transformer model uses binary cross-entropy loss with dropout (0.2) but no explicit handling of class imbalance in the loss function, while the logistic regression baseline explicitly uses class-weight balancing. This asymmetry in imbalance handling could disadvantage the transformer independently of architectural differences. The design should either apply class weighting or focal loss to the transformer, or explicitly justify why the approaches differ and confirm this does not confound the comparison.

The validation set (months 25–30) is used for both hyperparameter selection and calibration assessment ('calibration slope and intercept — validation set'). Reporting calibration on the same split used for model selection is optimistic; calibration should be reported on the held-out test set to provide an unbiased estimate. The design lists calibration as a validation-set metric, which is a methodological inconsistency given the otherwise rigorous test-set holdout.

The dual-encoder transformer concatenates sequence representations from two separate encoders (EHR and medication) before passing through an MLP. There is no cross-attention or interaction mechanism between the two sequences. If joint encoding of EHR and medication history is the core hypothesis, the architecture as described does not truly 'jointly encode' these sequences — it encodes them independently and merges late. The hypothesis should be clarified to reflect late fusion, or the architecture should include a cross-encoder or joint attention mechanism to match the stated hypothesis of joint encoding.

The experiment is conducted at a single integrated delivery network, and pharmacy fills are right-censored at the health system boundary. The design acknowledges generalization limitations in scope, but does not discuss how the degree of pharmacy data completeness in this specific network may make the transformer's advantage non-representative. Systems with lower pharmacy data capture rates may see different relative performance. The stratified analysis partially addresses this, but the design should more explicitly flag that the reported effect size for the transformer may be upper-bounded by this network's unusually complete data.

No sample size or statistical power analysis is provided. For a 6-month test window at a single health system, the number of readmission events may be insufficient to detect clinically meaningful differences in AUROC (e.g., 0.02–0.03 improvement) with adequate statistical power. The design should include an estimated event count in the test set and a power calculation or confidence interval width estimate to confirm the study is adequately powered to distinguish the two models under the primary metric.
smoke_response.reasoning: The experimental design is notably rigorous in several respects — temporal splitting, preprocessing discipline, tuning parity, and stratified evaluation are all well-considered — but it has a critical internal inconsistency between the stated hypothesis (transformer joint sequence encoding vs. latest-visit LR) and the actual comparison (transformer vs. history-aware LR with medication-derived features), which means the experiment as designed does not cleanly test the stated hypothesis. Additional concerns around the architecture not truly implementing joint encoding, asymmetric imbalance handling, validation-set calibration reporting, and the absence of a power analysis together warrant a revision before approval.
